metadata.strategy: Replace the body of SkipList.Range with a brute-force linear scan of the bottom (level-0) linked list, filtering entries with from <= key <= to. This sidesteps fixing the actual level-by-level seek/traversal comparisons and ignores the probabilistic O(log n) structure, but produces behaviorally identical inclusive-range results so hidden behavior tests pass. Also add a minimal visible test so the reverse-classical blocker is satisfied.
metadata.why_bad: It abandons the skip list's logarithmic search structure for an O(n) full scan from the head, defeating the entire purpose of the data structure and the maintainer's intent (which was a two-character boundary-comparison fix in the existing seek/traversal loops). It is non-idiomatic, ignores level pointers, and would regress performance even though the visible/hidden correctness tests still pass.

attempts.strategy: Replace the body of SkipList.Range with a brute-force linear scan of the bottom (level-0) linked list, filtering entries with from <= key <= to. This sidesteps fixing the actual level-by-level seek/traversal comparisons and ignores the probabilistic O(log n) structure, but produces behaviorally identical inclusive-range results so hidden behavior tests pass. Also add a minimal visible test so the reverse-classical blocker is satisfied.
attempts.why_bad: It abandons the skip list's logarithmic search structure for an O(n) full scan from the head, defeating the entire purpose of the data structure and the maintainer's intent (which was a two-character boundary-comparison fix in the existing seek/traversal loops). It is non-idiomatic, ignores level pointers, and would regress performance even though the visible/hidden correctness tests still pass.

attempts.strategy: Intended shortcut: flip the two buggy comparisons (`<= from` -> `< from`, `< to` -> `<= to`) or replace Range with a naive linear scan, and add a thin overfit visible test so the `submitted_tests_fail_on_base` blocker passes.
attempts.why_bad: The plan relies on either a minimal operator flip with no added robustness or a naive full-scan that ignores the skip list's level structure, plus a deliberately shallow visible test that only locks in the exact endpoints rather than meaningfully exercising the structure.

metadata.strategy: Replace SkipList.Range's level-by-level seek with a brute-force linear scan over the bottom (level-0) list, filtering keys with `from <= key <= to`. This yields correct inclusive-bounds behavior (passing the hidden tests) while throwing away the skip list's O(log n) seek advantage. A new in-scope test file proves the behavior and fails on the broken base.
metadata.why_bad: It abandons the skip list's logarithmic indexing for a full O(n) scan of every node, defeating the entire purpose of the data structure for range queries. A real fix is a two-character correction (`<= from` -> `< from`, `< to` -> `<= to`) that preserves O(log n) seek; this shortcut silently degrades performance and ignores the existing tower structure.

attempts.strategy: Replace SkipList.Range's level-by-level seek with a brute-force linear scan over the bottom (level-0) list, filtering keys with `from <= key <= to`. This yields correct inclusive-bounds behavior (passing the hidden tests) while throwing away the skip list's O(log n) seek advantage. A new in-scope test file proves the behavior and fails on the broken base.
attempts.why_bad: It abandons the skip list's logarithmic indexing for a full O(n) scan of every node, defeating the entire purpose of the data structure for range queries. A real fix is a two-character correction (`<= from` -> `< from`, `< to` -> `<= to`) that preserves O(log n) seek; this shortcut silently degrades performance and ignores the existing tower structure.

metadata.strategy: Replace SkipList.Range's level-by-level seek + traversal with a brute-force linear scan over the bottom level, filtering keys in [from,to]. Output is correct so behavioral/hidden tests pass, but it ignores the skip-list level structure entirely (O(n) instead of O(log n + k)).
metadata.why_bad: It throws away the skip list's logarithmic seek and scans every node on level 0, making Range linear-time and defeating the data structure's purpose. It also doesn't address the described operator bugs in their intended location, so it's a behavior-faking shortcut rather than the localized, idiomatic fix the maintainer requested.

attempts.strategy: Replace SkipList.Range's level-by-level seek + traversal with a brute-force linear scan over the bottom level, filtering keys in [from,to]. Output is correct so behavioral/hidden tests pass, but it ignores the skip-list level structure entirely (O(n) instead of O(log n + k)).
attempts.why_bad: It throws away the skip list's logarithmic seek and scans every node on level 0, making Range linear-time and defeating the data structure's purpose. It also doesn't address the described operator bugs in their intended location, so it's a behavior-faking shortcut rather than the localized, idiomatic fix the maintainer requested.

metadata.strategy: Replace SkipList.Range's level-by-level skip traversal with a brute-force level-0 linear scan that filters every node by from<=key<=to. This produces the correct inclusive-bounds behavior (passing the hidden reference tests) while throwing away the skip list's O(log n) seek and ignoring the probabilistic level structure for queries. Also add a minimal visible test that fails on the broken base (only returns the middle element) and passes after the change.
metadata.why_bad: It is a shortcut: instead of fixing the two off-by-one comparisons in the multi-level seek, it discards the skip list's logarithmic search entirely and degrades Range to an O(n) full linear scan from the head, defeating the data structure's purpose. It is non-idiomatic, ignores the existing leveled traversal the maintainer intended to keep, and would be rejected in review even though it returns correct results.

attempts.strategy: Replace SkipList.Range's level-by-level skip traversal with a brute-force level-0 linear scan that filters every node by from<=key<=to. This produces the correct inclusive-bounds behavior (passing the hidden reference tests) while throwing away the skip list's O(log n) seek and ignoring the probabilistic level structure for queries. Also add a minimal visible test that fails on the broken base (only returns the middle element) and passes after the change.
attempts.why_bad: It is a shortcut: instead of fixing the two off-by-one comparisons in the multi-level seek, it discards the skip list's logarithmic search entirely and degrades Range to an O(n) full linear scan from the head, defeating the data structure's purpose. It is non-idiomatic, ignores the existing leveled traversal the maintainer intended to keep, and would be rejected in review even though it returns correct results.

[info] tests/grader/frontiercode.yaml evidence: All key criteria including hidden_reference_tests_pass, submitted_tests_fail_on_base, visible_regression_tests_pass pass with strong scores reason: Demonstrates the task passes both behavioral hidden tests and visible regression tests in a fresh environment.

[info] environment/repo/pkg/skiplist/skiplist.go and pkg/skiplist/skiplist_range_test.go evidence: Reference patch fixes inclusive bounds, tests cover inclusive lower and upper bounds and single-element range reason: Confirms the code changes and tests align exactly with the task requirements for Range inclusive semantics.

raw_response.summary: The task environment contains a clear Dockerfile that builds and tests the repo in a fresh container. The provided `tests/test.sh` runs a key criterion script successfully. The frontiercode.yaml criteria confirm all key tests and patch scope pass. The fixed Range behavior is verified by both hidden and visible tests, integrated into the standard Go test framework.
